sourcePolicy: Public-safe generalized design heuristic. Contains no extracted assets, proprietary names, package identifiers, resource paths, copied numeric tables, screenshots, or private reports.

cards.title: Xianxia Idle Game Quality Bar
cards.summary: A xianxia idle game should feel elegant, readable, and emotionally aspirational rather than noisy, extractive, or overloaded with reward popups.
designPrinciples: Make cultivation fantasy the emotional core: quiet growth, breakthroughs, sect identity, artifacts, companions, realms, and dao choices.
Use monetization as optional acceleration, cosmetics, convenience, or collection expansion; avoid making direct power purchase the primary progression path.
Keep the base UI calm and legible. Reserve spectacle for breakthrough, rare loot, boss victory, new realm unlock, and story milestone moments.
Use layered xianxia visual language: ink, jade, metal, silk, mist, talisman paper, clouds, star charts, formation diagrams, alchemy fire, and artifact light.
Avoid a one-note palette. Pair restrained neutrals with meaningful accent colors for realm, element, rarity, faction, and danger states.
Separate gameplay clarity from decorative flourish. Numbers, timers, costs, and rewards must be readable before ornament is added.
Design every major system with a fantasy-facing name and a mechanics-facing model so the agent can generate both flavor and implementation.
uiRules: Use a stable mobile shell: top status strip, central activity/workspace, bottom navigation, contextual side actions, and modal panels only for focused decisions.
Limit simultaneous red-dot, reward, sale, and claim prompts. If more than three compete for attention, group them into a daily agenda panel.
Reward reveal screens should have three intensity levels: common claim, rare acquisition, and milestone ceremony.
Panels should use compact hierarchy: title, key stat delta, required resource, action button, secondary details. Avoid dense grids without filters or grouping.
Use motion to clarify state changes: resource gain, realm progress, breakthrough readiness, item upgrade, and failed requirement. Do not animate every decorative element.
characterArtRules: Generate characters as modular briefs: silhouette, robe/body layer, hair, face, weapon or artifact, aura, element motif, rarity treatment, and low-detail mobile fallback.
Differentiate role archetypes by silhouette and posture before color: sword cultivator, body refiner, talisman user, alchemist, formation master, beast tamer, demon path, and orthodox sect elder.
Paid cosmetics should change fantasy identity and presentation, not only add glow. Use garment structure, material, hair, accessory, aura, and stance changes.
Do not use reference-specific names, costume shapes, or identifiable motifs from private material. Create original faction and realm motifs for the target project.
sceneArtRules: Build scenes in layers: base environment, foreground frame, midground action space, background atmosphere, weather/fog, and UI-safe zones.
Use location identity to support progression: village, sect gate, cave dwelling, market, forbidden land, ancient battlefield, spirit mine, alchemy room, tower, secret realm, ascension platform.
For idle play, scenes should loop quietly and support long viewing sessions. Prefer subtle clouds, water, particles, banners, and lighting shifts over constant high-energy effects.
Major milestone scenes may use cinematic framing, but routine screens should remain fast, readable, and lightweight.
enemyArtRules: Enemy design should communicate region, threat tier, element, attack range, and reward type through silhouette and color accents.
Use readable families: beasts, ghosts, demons, puppets, rogue cultivators, ancient guardians, insects, plants, and corrupted artifacts.
Bosses need a unique silhouette plus one memorable mechanic-facing visual motif. Avoid making every boss a glow-heavy variant of the same body.
progressionRules: Model progression with named curves instead of copied values: realm growth, resource income, upgrade cost, battle power, drop chance, idle yield, and breakthrough risk.
Use soft caps, catch-up mechanics, and milestone unlocks to prevent whales from skipping the entire game loop.
Every paid accelerator should have a non-paid long-run path. Paid routes may save time or improve convenience, but should not invalidate strategy.
Balance should be tested with simulations for free, light-spender, and heavy-spender profiles before content is shipped.
cards.mgaiUsage: When generating a xianxia idle game plan, mgai should include a UI quality checklist, art direction brief, modular character prompt schema, scene prompt schema, enemy prompt schema, monetization fairness guardrails, and progression simulation tasks.
